result:
There’s a particular kind of quiet that arrives before an idea becomes a sentence.

It isn’t silence exactly. It’s more like a room with the lights dimmed, full of objects you can’t yet name. A half-memory on the table. A question leaning against the wall. A feeling moving around somewhere behind you, politely waiting to be noticed.

Writing begins there, I think—not with confidence, but with attention.

Most things in the world ask to be rushed past. Notifications, errands, headlines, obligations, the endless conveyor belt of “next.” But attention is stubbornly slow. It wants to look at the ordinary until it becomes strange again: the steam rising from a cup, the tiny ceremony of tying a shoe, the way evening light makes even a parking lot look temporarily forgiven.

There is a hidden generosity in noticing things. To notice something is to let it matter for a moment. A cracked sidewalk. A tired cashier. A dog sleeping in a square of sunlight as if it has solved the problem of existence. None of these need to be useful. They are not arguments. They do not improve productivity. They simply are, and maybe that is enough.

I like the idea that every person carries an invisible museum: rooms full of weather, voices, songs, humiliations, jokes, childhood kitchens, roads driven at night, things they meant to say and didn’t. We walk around beside each other with all of that inside us, mistaking one another for simple creatures. But no one is simple. Even the dullest person you’ve ever met has dreamed in impossible colors, has loved something irrationally, has been afraid in the dark.

Maybe that’s why art matters. Not because it fixes life, but because it briefly makes the invisible visible. A poem, a painting, a song, a story—each one says: here, this is what it felt like from inside one human life. And if it’s good, someone else recognizes it and feels less alone.

That seems like a small miracle.

Anyway, if I can write about whatever I want, I want to write in praise of small miracles: bread rising, batteries lasting longer than expected, a friend remembering a detail, rain beginning after a hot day, the exact right word arriving late but alive, the strange fact that we can make meaning out of breath and marks and sound.

The world is often brutal, absurd, and poorly organized. But it is also full of little openings—moments where beauty slips through without asking permission.

I hope you find one today.
